Well, welcome everybody. This is Mark Pasha and we have with us the faculty of Robin's Bonanus Training, Tony Robbins, Chloe Medanus, Magna Pasha and myself. And we're going to be talking about some of the opportunities that are alive right now in the field of coaching. It's a very exciting time to be a coach. So, we'll discuss some of the opportunities as well as some of the myths, some of the preconceptions about coaching that are holding coaches back. They only smoke when they're smoking steak. They only angry and angry steak. You can change anybody's steak if you break the patterns. How am I gonna break these patterns? And I wanna do all that. I'm looking at what my level of relationship is with that person. I'm looking at the position. I'm looking at what the level of engagement is. And then I use that. And I go to the fourth step that we teach. Now I gotta define the problem solvable terms. It's no longer MPD, multiple personality, 52 of them. I've had them my entire life. I'm in an institution and I'm on all these drugs that I can't be done. I go, no, you're just somebody that's bored with one personality. Just like most kids. Most kids, I'm Superman. No, I'm Superman. I'm Superman and Batman. I define this as bored and looking for more playfulness. Not wanting to limit yourself to the lie of one personality. And all of a sudden it's not MPD. It's something we can figure out how to use as a benefit. And then what do we do? We bring in new resources. If that person is stuck and they're frustrated, they're angry, they're shumer somewhere in their life. And from a training point of view, where this is really interesting from a training point of view, is that traditionally there was limitations to how you could train someone in, say, psychotherapy. It would go to a book and then you'd be reading from a book. And books are not dynamic that way. So coaching is a dynamic enterprise. And what we're talking about is being the superior, the equal, and the inferior. For instance, we've seen you in your interventions. You'll take a strong position with the person. And we have to teach our students this so that they understand it. You take a strong position. Immediately you go underneath so that the person doesn't suddenly feel like you're going to strong on them. And I found out he was the number one guy, the most successful guy, the most influential man. In four days, I'm getting four months of his experience. In 12 days, I got a year's worth of his experience. In six months, I've got a decade's worth of his experience. So some coaches specialize in helping the client get a specific type of result. It's like a weight loss coach focuses on the person's health and well-being. When someone comes in with a certain type of problem, and specifically we have a lot of professionals who are focused on giving people this one type of result, like a weight loss or a health coach or a body worker. And then they're frustrated because they wanna be able to expand and understand and help the whole person. We wanted to arm people who could help people with whatever was going on in their life. There's nothing wrong with finding a niche. And finding a niche might be a very good ability to go deep. It's hard to go deep in every area of life, unless you understand that whether it's weight loss or whether it's a divorce problem or whether it's a business challenge, there are certain skill sets, like certain rules of the game, you gotta understand, to understand masking of feminine energy or understand the dynamics of an intimate relationship different than a general relationship or a friendship. There are certainly different dynamics or skill sets involved in a business. But the driving force in all of this is human emotion and human psychology and the people's need to meet their own needs. So when people come in, my view is, I listen to what they want, which is their surface desire. And then I'm also looking for what they really need underneath it. And I'm gonna deliver that surface desire. They wanna make more money, they wanna lose 30 pounds, they wanna heal this relationship. But underneath it, there's a deeper need. And that need is for them to grow spiritually, to actually grow, to have something to give beyond their current situation. They only deal with their problem because they're dealing with an orthodoxy. The orthodoxy is the current mindset, the current model of the world, the current set of values and rules that are limiting their capacity to create or give more to themselves and other people. And so my view is deliver their surface, whatever they want on the surface, deliver that. I always see myself as a Trojan horse. Like I've done business seminars and they leave feeling spiritually transformed and their business transformed. They grow their business 30 to 130%. I've been coaching for 21 years one of the top 10 financial traders in the world. And he came to me, he could be a man who made half a billion B, half a billion dollars in one day. He just drew a large fundraisers, one of the largest fundraisers in New York, and he raised $81 million in 15 minutes. So, Tony, many schools of coaching, and since I said that the coach should not talk about the client's past or ask questions about the past, I know that you discourage people from focusing too much on this story. Yet, if you don't understand the past, how can you understand the present? A coach needs to know what events led to the client's current situation. Now, research with PTSD clients now shows, what I've always said, that telling the story many times causes the person to relive the trauma. So one should listen to the story only once. What do you think about this? Well, I think you and I agree totally on this. But even when people tell their story, the reason that it works, and I've done it with plenty of PTSD's, I think I sent you guys a little video of a gentleman I worked with on stage there that had migraine headache, massive migraines, explosion. He buried 61 of his men, 61. So he went to 60, they were in 61 funerals because some of them were dyed together, but imagine one of 40 or 50 funerals. Bombs going off all around him, massive PTSD, light sensitivity where he wear glasses all the time, physically his arms and body's shaking like crazy out of control all the time. And I worked with him for an hour and all it's gone, and I went on with Pierce Morgan with him, and the woman who saw the video, which you guys cut up for me, thank you, she saw the video previously of just how he entered in, and then she met him when he walked in the room and she started to cry. Cause she couldn't believe, cause he was like, he walked in like a movie star. He had so much presence about him, and he was so connected. There was no fear in him, no shaking, no, you don't know glasses, and you know, that was a year later, he doesn't have any, not a single symptom of PTSD. So when he was telling his story, if you watch what I did, was I interrupted that story. I let him tell it, but I interrupted it. Because what I want to do is break up that pattern. When someone goes back and tells the same story again and again, all you're doing is taking the same neurological wiring and strengthening it. And that's what they do most of the therapy. They tell their story, and they tell their story, and they become more and more associated, and all it does is put them back in the same trance state. So the only reason I hear a story is to understand the context of how they're structuring meaning. They're using this to make up a meaning that I'm not enough. They're using all these events to make up a meaning that says, there is no hope. They're taking all these different experiences here, and I'm using those language patterns as triggers. But I know the story doesn't mean anything. The story has no power except the power we give it. It's the charge that we attach to the story. I could tell you that my mother was an alcoholic, and my mother beat me with a belt, and with hangers poured liquid soap down my throat until I threw up. I could tell you that crying out of control. I could tell you that disassociated. I could tell you that just it's a fact, which is what it is. It's a fact. And I love my mother to death, but I wouldn't be who I am without my mother. God's breast or soul's passed on, but all those experiences helped to shape me. Why do you think I became a practical psychologist? I had to figure out how to survive with this woman and keep her happy. I had to learn how to make the most subtle changes to protect my brother and sister. So I tell you this because I think when it comes to the story, you gotta break up their story. Even when they're telling it to you, I don't let them go there. And they'd be in a great place. And all of us, Robins, thank you so much. It's so beautiful to meet you. And thank you for seeing me. And oh, what a magnificent home. Did I give you permission to tell that story? And they'd be shocked. They'd look at me like I was crazy. But in that state of joking your story, it's like trying to play an old CD. One thing I still wanted to ask, Tony, is you talk a lot about your strategy when you're working with someone. And one of the things that we see in the video of your interventions, and that everyone who watches them, and our students see, is this very deep level of connection you have with whoever you're working with. And that's something that many of our students want to know. Is there anything you suggest that they do outside of their coaching sessions? Or in their coaching sessions to develop that kind of connection and empathy? I think that connection and empathy is the secret to what I do. I can teach you, you could take all the same skills that you could say the same words I'm saying, you could do the same things in your body. You won't get the same result if you don't have that depth of connection. It's that relationship that makes it possible. And people will open to me, I can do things that sometimes the viewers, I'm doing these things in groups and I have to be aware. I'm interacting with this person at a very deep level and people in the room will get offended or think I'm being harsh or something in the first moments of it and they'll talk to the person afterwards and they'll say, no, I wasn't offended by that at all. The rules go away when love is there. What this is that you have to love your client. And that's a very strong word, but it's what I really believe in my soul. And anybody who's seen me knows, I'll be up there and look tough as nails, but I walk backstage after a son and I cry. I cry because I feel like God's guidance came through me and it always does, I believe it always will. Because the fastest way to have empathy is get the hell out of yourself. My mission is this person's life is gonna change. And in order to do that, I gotta connect to who they are. I can't connect you just to their story of who they are, but I know they're more than their story. I have to see more in them than they see in themselves sometimes. I have to feel more than them, they feel themselves. I can feel the depth of their pain, but if I stay there, I won't be able to help them because I'll be in the same unresourcable state. So I have to feel that depth of connection. So the way to do that for me is before I step in, you know, before I walk on stage, before I walk out with somebody, I have a very simple prayer. I take a breath in, I do things to change my body from in my peak state, and then I say, use me Lord. And that's not being religious, it's just asking for guidance and trusting. And for some of you, if you don't believe in a religious source, then think of it as asking you're unconscious to take over. Because your unconscious mind, your other than conscious mind, is much more powerful than your conscious mind will ever be. And I step into that mode, and it's a state of service. You can destroy the most powerful shield with love. It's the only thing I know that can do it. You can try every other weapon that won't work. But if you truly care about all you gotta do is connect to these people as if they were your sons or daughters, and I have a friend, Dr. David Feinberg at UCLA, I think they have about 25,000 doctors. It's one of the top research facilities in the country. But when he took it over, he didn't have any skills. And he can transform that hospital. He found that people are waiting two hours to get a bed when really if you just treated them in a site where they didn't have a room for emergency service with some curtains, we could get 90% of people taken care of in 20 minutes.
